name: SEQUENCE ECHO
conditioned_path: 'only 90s kids remember' nostalgia-chain posts that bait recall of childhood sequences
mechanic: A colored tile sequence flashes one by one; the thumb must replay the sequence in exact order by tapping the same tiles. Each correct echo adds one more tile to the next round, growing the span until a miss ends the run and prints the final sequence length.
video_trick: near-perfection itch — the last tile missed haunts the rewatch

name: FLIP PAIRS
conditioned_path: nostalgia-bait photo pairs ('here vs now') that farm the match-recognition reflex
mechanic: A grid of face-down cards; flip two at a time to find matching pairs. All cards are cleared in one session — the score is total flip count, lower is better, daring friends to beat the memory efficiency.
video_trick: competitive rank — 'I cleared it in 18 flips, beat that'

name: SPOT THE CHANGE
conditioned_path: 'before and after' transformation clips that train the eye to hunt for what changed
mechanic: Two nearly identical screens flash alternating; tap the element that changed before the cycle repeats. Each round the change shrinks and the cycle speeds — the recall reflex is exercised, not just baited.
video_trick: play-along comment bait — 'pause and spot it before I reveal'

name: WORD SPAN
conditioned_path: 'how many can you remember?' recall-challenge posts that spread via score-sharing
mechanic: A list of words flashes for 3 seconds, then vanishes; tap the words that appeared from a larger set of distractors. The list grows by one word each round until a miss prints the working-memory span — the honest score the brain earned.
video_trick: benchmark ego bait — 'I hit 9, what\'s yours?'

name: GRID GHOST
conditioned_path: hidden-object / 'what\'s different' clip format that parks the pattern in short-term memory
mechanic: A grid of symbols lights up briefly, then goes dark; tap the cells that were lit in any order. Every correct tap scores; every wrong tap ends the round. The lit pattern grows each level, exercising spatial working memory.
video_trick: near-perfection itch

name: NOSTALGIA NAME
conditioned_path: 'name it if you remember it' challenge posts that weaponize the recall reflex for shares
mechanic: A blurred or pixelated icon from a shared-era cultural object (game, toy, show) fades in slowly; tap the correct name from three choices before it fully reveals. Score is how early you tapped — the earlier the blur-stage, the more points.
video_trick: ego bait — prove you remember faster than friends

name: ORDER REPLAY
conditioned_path: viral 'put these in order' ranking challenges that bait debate and recall
mechanic: Five items appear in random order for 4 seconds, then scramble; drag them back into the exact original order from memory. Miss-order distance is the score — lower is better, and rounds get longer lists.
video_trick: participation-comment-bait — 'I got it perfect, did you?'

name: COLOR CHAIN
conditioned_path: trending color-sequence challenges that spread because scores are socially comparable
mechanic: A chain of colored dots pulses in sequence; replay the chain by tapping the dots in the same order. Each correct replay adds one dot to the chain. The longest unbroken chain is the dare unit sent to friends.
video_trick: competitive rank

name: FACE RECALL
conditioned_path: 'do you remember them?' celebrity throwback posts that farm recognition memory
mechanic: Three illustrated faces flash for 2 seconds; a lineup of six appears and the thumb must tap the three shown. Faces share features to confuse recognition. Score is correct picks; dare-link sends the lineup to friends.
video_trick: ego bait — 'I got all three, think you can?'

name: MEMORY TRAIL
conditioned_path: path-tracing / route-memory challenge clips where watching the trail is the hook
mechanic: A dot traces a winding path across the canvas for 3 seconds; then trace the same path from memory with one finger. Deviation from the original path is scored in pixels — the PB is the tightest trace.
video_trick: mastery flex — route memory tested with a real score

name: NUMBER VAULT
conditioned_path: digit-span recall games popularized as 'memory IQ' viral clips
mechanic: A number string flashes for 1.5 seconds per two digits; then type it back in full. Length increases by one digit each round. Score is the longest string correctly recalled — the honest working-memory digit span.
video_trick: benchmark ego bait — digit span as social currency

name: SHADOW MATCH
conditioned_path: 'guess the silhouette' nostalgia clips that bait the recognition snap with partial cues
mechanic: A silhouette flashes for one second; six choices appear and the thumb taps the match. Silhouettes get more ambiguous each round and choices more similar. Streak ends on a miss — the brain\'s recognition memory exercised at real speed.
video_trick: play-along comment bait — 'did you get it before the reveal?'

name: DECODE RECALL
conditioned_path: cryptic-clue / code-recall viral challenges that prove memory under pressure
mechanic: A symbol-to-letter cipher is shown for 10 seconds; it hides and a word appears in symbols. Decode it from memory by tapping the letters. Longer words and more cipher entries ramp the working-memory load each round.
video_trick: anticipation agony — 'can you hold the cipher long enough to crack the word?'

name: SCENE REWIND
conditioned_path: 'spot what changed between these two screenshots' nostalgia-diff posts
mechanic: A scene of illustrated objects shows for 4 seconds; it resets with 3 objects removed and 2 added. Tap the objects that were NOT in the original scene. Score is correct identifications; dare-link prints the change count to beat.
video_trick: near-perfection itch

name: RHYTHM REPEAT
conditioned_path: drum-pattern / handclap recall challenges where the rhythm plants in short-term memory
mechanic: A tap-rhythm pattern plays as haptic pulses on screen; the thumb replays it by tapping. Pattern length grows each round. Miss-timing by more than 100ms ends the streak — sequence recall through the motor channel.
video_trick: participation-comment-bait — 'can you feel the pattern and send it back?'

name: MAP RECALL
conditioned_path: viral 'place the flag / country quiz' recall posts that weaponize geography memory for shares
mechanic: A simple illustrated map appears for 5 seconds with 5 marked spots; the map fades and the thumb must drag pins to the remembered positions. Distance from each original spot is scored — the dare unit is total error in map-units.
video_trick: competitive rank — 'total error 47, think you can beat it?'

name: MISSING TILE
conditioned_path: 'what\'s wrong with this picture?' change-blindness clips that make the viewer feel foolish then share
mechanic: A full grid of colored tiles shows for 3 seconds; one tile vanishes and the grid reshuffles. Tap the empty slot where the missing tile belongs. The grid grows each level and reshuffles more aggressively.
video_trick: shock-honesty — 'change blindness is real, I missed it on round 4'

name: STORY SEQUENCE
conditioned_path: 'put this story in order' narrative-recall posts that spread on nostalgia for shared moments
mechanic: Four illustrated story panels flash in order for 2 seconds each; they scramble and the thumb drags them back into the correct narrative sequence. Error count is zero or greater — a perfect run is the only dare worth sharing.
video_trick: completion-satisfaction — the clean reorder lands like a solved puzzle

name: RECALL GARDEN
conditioned_path: 'on this day' memory-bait posts that pull the nostalgia reflex into a feed loop
mechanic: A soft sequence of illustrated memory-motes (shapes, colors, small icons) floats past one at a time; after the last one, the thumb gently taps each that appeared, in any order. No timer, no miss penalty — the session ends when all are acknowledged and a garden tile blooms.
video_trick: calm completion — the recall loop that resolves instead of repeating

name: ECHO FADE
conditioned_path: nostalgia-ASMR 'remember this sound?' posts that park a memory and reopen it gently
mechanic: A short visual sequence pulses onto the screen, one element at a time, slowly; after it fades, the thumb traces the elements back in order at any pace. Each correct touch plays a soft chime. The sequence is short enough to always succeed — the exercise is pure gentle recall, not a test.
video_trick: calm completion
